facade_colour: dark green/olive painted storefront, red brick upper

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (8 Kensington Avenue), Converted House-form, Semi-detached (10 (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property at 10 Kensington Avenue also has value for its modifications to accommodate a commercial conversion through a one-storey commercial addition. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a pair, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, polychromatic brickwork, round arched attic windows, and flat headed windows at the second storey. The property at 8 Kensington Avenue has also retained its first storey bay window. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

facade_description: 10 Kensington Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 1, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include bay window, decorative brick, original windows. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property at 10 Kensington Avenue also has value for its modifications to accommodate a commercial conversion through a one-storey commercial addition. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a pair, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, polychromatic brickwork, round arched attic windows, and flat headed windows at the second storey. The property at 8 Kensington Avenue has also retained its first storey bay window. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.
